section.title: Trust in the LORD
section.sectionRationale: The third discourse gathers a string of connected appeals around one center, verses 5 to 8, trust in the LORD with all your heart. Around that center the father urges loving devotion, honors God with wealth, welcomes His discipline, praises wisdom's cosmic worth, promises safety, and closes with practical love of neighbor and the LORD's opposition to the proud.
section.thesis: A wise life is not built on self reliant understanding but on whole hearted trust in the LORD, a trust that shapes worship, welcomes discipline, prizes wisdom above riches, and works itself out in concrete kindness toward others.
section.frameNote: Six movements: bind loving devotion and faithfulness to yourself, verses 1 to 4; trust in the LORD, the chapter's center, verses 5 to 8; honor the LORD with wealth and welcome His discipline, verses 9 to 12; the surpassing worth of wisdom, verses 13 to 20; safety for the one who keeps sound judgment, verses 21 to 26; and practical love of neighbor closing with the LORD's grace to the humble, verses 27 to 35.

verses.text: My son, do not forget my teaching, but let your heart keep my commandments;
for they will add length to your days, years and peace to your life.
Never let {{chesed|loving devotion}} or faithfulness leave you; bind them around your neck, write them on the tablet of your heart.
Then you will find favor and high regard in the sight of God and man.
Trust in the LORD with all your heart, and lean not on your own understanding;
in all your ways acknowledge Him, and He will make your paths straight.
Be not wise in your own eyes; {{yirah|fear}} the LORD and turn away from evil.
This will bring healing to your body and refreshment to your bones.

units.label: Honor the LORD, welcome His discipline
units.note: wealth and correction both belong to Him

verses.text: Honor the LORD with your wealth and with the firstfruits of all your harvest;
then your barns will be filled with plenty, and your vats will overflow with new wine.
My son, do not reject the {{musar|discipline}} of the LORD, and do not loathe His rebuke;
for the LORD disciplines the one He loves, as does a father the son in whom he delights.
Blessed is the man who finds {{chokmah|wisdom}}, the man who acquires understanding,
for she is more profitable than silver, and her gain is better than fine gold.
She is more precious than rubies; nothing you desire compares with her.
Long life is in her right hand; in her left hand are riches and honor.
All her ways are pleasant, and all her paths are peaceful.
She is a tree of life to those who embrace her, and those who lay hold of her are blessed.
The LORD founded the earth by wisdom and established the heavens by understanding.
By His knowledge the watery depths were broken open, and the clouds dripped with dew.

units.label: Safety for the one who keeps sound judgment

verses.text: My son, do not lose sight of this: Preserve sound judgment and discernment.
They will be life to your soul and adornment to your neck.
Then you will go on your way in safety, and your foot will not stumble.
When you lie down, you will not be afraid; when you rest, your sleep will be sweet.
Do not fear sudden danger or the ruin that overtakes the wicked,
for the LORD will be your confidence and will keep your foot from the snare.

units.label: Love your neighbor, and the LORD's grace to the humble

verses.text: Do not withhold good from the deserving when it is within your power to act.
Do not tell your neighbor, "Come back tomorrow and I will provide," when you already have the means.
Do not devise evil against your neighbor, for he trustfully dwells beside you.
Do not accuse a man without cause, when he has done you no harm.
Do not envy a violent man or choose any of his ways;
for the LORD detests the perverse, but He is a friend to the upright.
The curse of the LORD is on the house of the wicked, but He blesses the home of the righteous.
He mocks the mockers, but gives grace to the humble.
The wise will inherit honor, but fools are held up to shame.

chesed.gloss: Loving devotion, a rich word covering love, kindness, faithfulness, mercy, and loyalty to a covenant. It is most often used of God's covenant love, but here the son is told to bind it around his own neck, letting God's covenant love shape his own character toward others.

yirah.gloss: The fear of the LORD, reverent trust that turns away from evil. Paired here with not being wise in your own eyes, it marks humility before God as the opposite of self reliant understanding.

musar.gloss: Discipline or correction. Verses 11 to 12 reframe discipline as evidence of fatherly love rather than divine hostility, a text the New Testament quotes directly in Hebrews 12:5-6 to comfort believers under hardship.

chokmah.gloss: Wisdom, praised here as more valuable than silver, gold, or rubies, and as the very instrument by which the LORD founded the earth and the heavens, verses 19 to 20. She is pictured as a tree of life to those who embrace her.

basic.context: This chapter strings together some of the most beloved lines in Proverbs, do not lean on your own understanding, honor the LORD with your wealth, do not despise His discipline, wisdom is more precious than rubies, and He gives grace to the humble. Read as a whole, it teaches that a life built on trusting God, not on self sufficient cleverness, is the true path to safety, blessing, and a good name.
basic.hermeneutics: The chapter moves like a string of pearls, each section connected to whole hearted trust in God. Verses 5 to 8 are its literal and thematic center: trust, not self reliant understanding, is the operating principle beneath worship, discipline, wisdom, safety, and neighbor love alike. None of this is magic; it describes the normal shape of a God trusting life, not a formula that removes all hardship, as verses 11 to 12 make explicit by assuming that God's own children will be disciplined, sometimes painfully.
basic.originalLanguages: Chesed, loving devotion, is the rich covenant word for God's own faithful love, here commanded as a trait to bind around one's own neck. Musar, discipline, in verses 11 to 12 is reframed as proof of fatherly love, a connection so important the author of Hebrews quotes it directly. And chokmah, wisdom, is praised as more valuable than any treasure and as the very means by which the LORD founded the world, verses 19 to 20.
basic.typology: Wisdom as a tree of life, verse 18, and as the one through whom the world was founded, verses 19 to 20, anticipates the New Testament's description of Christ as the one through whom and for whom all things were created, Colossians 1:16, and as the wisdom of God, 1 Corinthians 1:24. The discipline of a loving father, verses 11 to 12, points to God's fatherly discipline of every child He receives, Hebrews 12:5 to 6.
basic.christ: This chapter's center, trust in the LORD rather than your own understanding, finds its deepest ground in Christ, who trusted the Father perfectly, even to the point of death, and who is Himself the wisdom by which the world was made, Colossians 1:16 to 17, 1 Corinthians 1:24. The discipline commended in verses 11 to 12 is quoted in Hebrews 12:5 to 6 as proof that God treats believers as true sons, disciplined because of, not despite, His love shown fully at the cross.
basic.god: God is the one worth trusting completely, the one who deserves the firstfruits of all we have, the Father who disciplines those He loves, and the giver of a wisdom so vast that the earth and heavens themselves rest on it. He is also humble toward the humble, giving grace to them while opposing the proud, a truth quoted twice in the New Testament, James 4:6 and 1 Peter 5:5.
basic.crossReferences: Hebrews 12:5 to 6 quotes verses 11 to 12 directly on the discipline of a loving Father. James 4:6 and 1 Peter 5:5 quote verse 34 on grace for the humble. Colossians 1:16 to 17 presents Christ as the one through whom all things were created, echoing wisdom's role in verses 19 to 20. 1 Corinthians 1:24 calls Christ the wisdom of God.
basic.oneStory: Trust in the LORD, not self reliant understanding, is the thread running through this whole chapter, worship, discipline, wisdom, safety, and neighbor love all flow from it. That same trust finds its fullest object in Christ, the wisdom by whom the world was made and the Son who trusted His Father even to the cross, so that all who trust in Him receive both fatherly discipline and unearned grace.

context.literary: A chain of short units bound together by a shared theme rather than one continuous argument, moving from personal devotion, verses 1 to 4, to its center on trust, verses 5 to 8, then outward to worship, discipline, wisdom's worth, personal safety, and neighbor love, closing on the LORD's opposition to the proud and grace to the humble, verse 34, a line the New Testament quotes twice.
context.historical: Firstfruits offerings, verse 9, and the ordinary hazards of ancient life, sudden danger, unjust accusation, envy of violent men, verses 25 and 29 to 31, situate this wisdom in the everyday economic and social world of ancient Israel, where trusting God had very practical, financial, and relational shape.
context.canonical: Verses 11 to 12 and 34 are both quoted directly in the New Testament, Hebrews 12:5 to 6 and James 4:6 with 1 Peter 5:5, showing how deeply this chapter's teaching on discipline and humility shaped later biblical reflection on the Christian life.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To press whole hearted trust in the LORD as the operating principle beneath every area of life named in the chapter, worship, correction, the pursuit of wisdom, personal safety, and dealings with neighbors, so that trust, not self sufficient cleverness, becomes the defining posture of the wise.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Trusting God, honoring Him with wealth, and loving one's neighbor are given as direct commands. But some of the surrounding promises, straight paths in verse 6, healing in verse 8, filled barns in verse 10, describe the normal, general blessing that flows from a trusting life under God, not an unconditional guarantee against all hardship; the chapter itself assumes real discipline and real danger are still part of that same trusting life, verses 11 to 12, 25.

debates.question: Does verse 9, honoring the LORD with wealth for full barns in return, teach a guaranteed prosperity?

views.label: A description of God's normal, generous pattern
views.case: Verse 10 states a real, general connection between honoring God with resources and material blessing, consistent with the broader wisdom tradition that generosity tends toward flourishing.

views.label: A general proverb, not an unconditional formula
views.case: Proverbs elsewhere shows the righteous suffering and the wicked prospering, Proverbs 13:23, Ecclesiastes 7:15, so this verse describes the ordinary tendency of honoring God with resources, not a mechanical transaction that guarantees wealth to every giver.

debates.bearing: Read within the whole of Proverbs and Scripture, verse 9 to 10 commends generous, God honoring stewardship as wise and normally fruitful, without turning giving into a guaranteed exchange for riches, a distortion the prosperity gospel makes of texts like this one.

debates.question: How should verses 11 to 12 shape a Christian's view of hardship?

views.label: Hardship as evidence of God's fatherly love
views.case: Hebrews 12:5 to 6 quotes this passage to comfort suffering believers, arguing that discipline itself is proof of sonship, not divine rejection.

views.label: A warning against resenting or despising correction
views.case: The command is as much about the son's response, do not reject, do not loathe, as about the fact of discipline itself, aiming at humble acceptance rather than bitterness or self pity.

debates.bearing: Both readings work together, the passage teaches that God's discipline is loving, and it calls for a humble, trusting response rather than resentment, exactly the pastoral use Hebrews makes of it.

study.originalLanguages: Chesed, loving devotion, names God's own covenant faithfulness, yet the son is told to bind it around his own neck, letting divine love shape his character. Yirah, the fear of the LORD, verse 7, stands opposite to being wise in one's own eyes, framing humility as trust's natural posture. Musar, discipline, verse 11, is reframed from something to be resented into proof of a father's delight, a connection Hebrews 12:5 to 6 quotes directly. And chokmah, wisdom, verses 13 to 20, is praised as more valuable than any treasure and as the LORD's own instrument in founding the earth and the heavens.

typology.type: Wisdom as the LORD's instrument in founding the earth, Proverbs 3:19-20
typology.fulfillment: Christ, in whom and through whom all things were created, and in whom all things hold together, Colossians 1:16-17.

typology.type: Wisdom as a tree of life, Proverbs 3:18
typology.fulfillment: Christ, in whom believers find true and lasting life, and the tree of life restored in the new creation, Revelation 22:2.

typology.type: The discipline of a loving father, Proverbs 3:11-12
typology.fulfillment: God's fatherly discipline of every believing son, quoted directly in Hebrews 12:5-6.

study.christ: The chapter's center, whole hearted trust in the LORD rather than self reliant understanding, verses 5 to 6, finds its perfect human expression in Jesus, who trusted His Father completely, even in Gethsemane and at the cross. Wisdom's cosmic role in founding the earth and the heavens, verses 19 to 20, is fulfilled in Christ, through whom and for whom all things were created, Colossians 1:16, and who is called the wisdom of God, 1 Corinthians 1:24. And the discipline of a loving father, verses 11 to 12, is quoted in Hebrews 12:5 to 6 as evidence that every trial a believer faces comes from the same fatherly hand that did not spare His own Son for us.
study.god: God is worthy of complete trust, worthy of the firstfruits of all we have, and a Father who disciplines those He loves rather than abandoning them. He is also the architect of the universe by wisdom, and He is near to the humble even while He actively opposes the proud, a pattern the New Testament repeats as a summary of how God relates to human pride and humility.

crossReferences.note: Quotes Proverbs 3:11-12 directly to comfort believers under God's fatherly discipline.
Quotes Proverbs 3:34, God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble.
Quotes the same verse, urging humility because God gives grace to the humble.
Christ as the one through whom and for whom all things were created and in whom all things hold together, echoing wisdom's cosmic role in verses 19-20.

study.oneStory: Proverbs 3 calls for trust in the LORD over self reliant understanding as the root of a wise life, worship, discipline, wisdom's pursuit, safety, and neighbor love. That same trust finds its object and its model in Christ, the wisdom through whom the world was founded and the Son who trusted His Father perfectly, so that everyone who leans not on their own understanding but on Him receives both loving discipline and undeserved grace.

sermon.title: Wisdom: How To Get It
sermon.why: Keller preaches directly through Proverbs 3:1-8 and 11-12, the heart of this chapter, unpacking what it means to trust in the LORD with all your heart and to receive His discipline as the love of a father.

quiz.q: What is the chapter's central command, stated in verses 5 to 6?

options.text: Trust in the LORD with all your heart, and do not lean on your own understanding.
options.note: This is the exact wording and thematic center of the whole chapter.

options.text: Work hard and trust your own judgment above all else.
options.note: This is the opposite of what the passage commands.

options.text: Seek wealth first, and wisdom will follow.
options.note: The chapter reverses this order, honoring God and trusting Him comes first, and material blessing is described as flowing from that, not the other way around.

options.text: Trust experts and teachers more than Scripture.
options.note: Listening to teaching matters elsewhere in Proverbs, but this passage's specific command is to trust the LORD Himself, not human experts as such.

quiz.q: How does the chapter describe God's discipline in verses 11 to 12?

options.text: As proof of fatherly love, like a father who disciplines the son he delights in.
options.note: This is quoted directly in Hebrews 12:5-6 to comfort believers under hardship.

options.text: As a sign that God has rejected the one being disciplined.
options.note: The passage explicitly says the opposite, discipline flows from love, not rejection.

options.text: As something only unbelievers experience.
options.note: The image is of a father and a beloved son, describing God's people, not those outside His family.

options.text: As a punishment with no relational meaning.
options.note: This misses the passage's emphasis on fatherly delight and love as the reason for the discipline, not bare punishment.

quiz.q: How is wisdom described in verses 13 to 20?

options.text: More precious than rubies, a tree of life, and the LORD's own instrument in founding the earth and heavens.
options.note: All three images appear directly in this section.

options.text: A minor and optional virtue.
options.note: The passage instead exalts wisdom as supremely valuable and cosmically significant.

options.text: Available only to kings and the wealthy.
options.note: The passage addresses 'my son' broadly, not a wealthy elite.

options.text: Useful mainly for financial success.
options.note: Financial gain is mentioned, verse 14, but the passage's larger claim is about wisdom's incomparable worth and cosmic role, not merely money.

quiz.q: What does verse 34 say about the LORD's dealings with the proud and the humble, and where is it quoted in the New Testament?

options.text: He mocks the mockers but gives grace to the humble, quoted in James 4:6 and 1 Peter 5:5.
options.note: This is the exact verse and its two New Testament citations.

options.text: He treats the proud and humble exactly the same.
options.note: The verse draws a sharp contrast between the two, not sameness.

options.text: He rewards pride if it produces results.
options.note: This directly contradicts the verse, which says He mocks the mockers.

options.text: It is quoted once, in Romans, on justification.
options.note: Close on the idea of a New Testament citation, but the actual quotations are in James 4:6 and 1 Peter 5:5, on humility, not in Romans on justification.

quiz.q: How does wisdom's role in founding the earth, verses 19 to 20, point to Christ?

options.text: Christ is the one through whom and for whom all things were created, and in whom all things hold together.
options.note: Colossians 1:16-17 makes this connection directly, and 1 Corinthians 1:24 calls Christ the wisdom of God.

options.text: It shows wisdom as a purely human trait with no connection to Christ.
options.note: The passage's language of founding the earth points beyond a merely human virtue to something the New Testament applies to Christ Himself.

options.text: It teaches that Christ is less significant than divine wisdom.
options.note: The New Testament instead identifies Christ Himself as the wisdom of God, not something lesser than it.

options.text: It is a poetic image with no theological weight.
options.note: This understates how directly the New Testament builds on this very image to describe Christ's role in creation.

questions: Verse 5 calls for trust in the LORD with all your heart, not leaning on your own understanding. Where in your life right now are you leaning most heavily on your own reasoning instead of trusting God?
Verses 11 to 12 reframe discipline as evidence of a father's love. What hardship or correction are you currently tempted to resent, and how might seeing it as fatherly love change your response?
The chapter prizes wisdom above silver, gold, and rubies. If someone looked at how you spend your time and money, would they conclude that you value wisdom that highly?

Verses 27 to 31 call for concrete kindness and honesty toward neighbors, not withholding good, not delaying help you could give now. Who is someone you could bless today rather than putting off until tomorrow?
Verse 34 says God gives grace to the humble but opposes the proud. Where do you need to trade self reliant pride for humble trust in God this week?

translationNotes.note: This will bring healing renders a Hebrew phrase literally meaning to your navel, an idiom for the whole body's vitality; some translations render the second half as medicine to your bones.

The Septuagint reading, quoted in Hebrews 12:5-6, reads slightly differently, and do not lose heart when He rebukes you, and He flogs every son He receives, a wording the New Testament author follows directly.
The Septuagint reading, quoted in James 4:6 and 1 Peter 5:5, reads the Lord opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble, the exact form both New Testament letters cite.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Proverbs 3:1-35. One em dash present in the source at verse 28 was rewritten using a comma to comply with the no dash rule; the wording is otherwise unchanged and complete.
